title: Pea and mint soup
subtitle: Fresh, bright, and ready in fifteen minutes
excerpt: Frozen peas blended with mint, a little stock, and a swirl of crème fraîche. One of the quickest soups there is, good hot or cold, and an excellent use for frozen peas.
sourceNotes: Standard British spring soup, widely published from the 1980s onward. Frozen peas as a year-round substitute for fresh peas became a British domestic staple in the 1960s and has been used in soups ever since.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding the crème fraîche. The soup keeps frozen for up to three months. Defrost and reheat gently, stirring in crème fraîche when hot.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes and freezes beautifully in large quantities.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for two days. The mint colour fades slightly but the flavour stays bright.

glossaryTerms.definition: To plunge food briefly into boiling water, then transfer it immediately into cold water to stop the cooking. Preserves colour and texture, particularly in green vegetables.

content.text: Pea and mint soup is fifteen minutes from start to finish. Frozen peas are already
and need very little cooking: a brief simmer in stock, then into the blender with a handful of fresh mint. The key to keeping the colour vivid is speed: overcook the peas and the soup turns khaki.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft.
Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for 1 minute. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock and bring to a simmer.
Add {{peas-frozen}} of frozen peas straight from the freezer. Return to a simmer and cook for exactly 3 minutes. Do not overcook.
Transfer the soup to a jug blender in batches. Add the mint leaves. Blend until completely smooth.
Return to the pan over a low heat. Stir in {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Add a small spoonful of crème fraîche to each bowl and a mint leaf on top.

items.symptom: Soup is khaki rather than vivid green
items.cause: The peas were cooked for too long, destroying the chlorophyll.
items.fix: Three minutes for frozen peas is enough. If the soup has gone dull, try adding a handful of fresh raw spinach leaves into the blender with the cooked peas: they boost the colour back.

items.cause: Old or cheap frozen peas with a lot of starch.
items.fix: Use a brand of frozen peas with a high sugar content (the packaging sometimes states 'garden peas' or 'petits pois'). The freshness of the peas matters for flavour.

items.cause: A stick blender does not produce as fine a result as a jug blender for pea soup.
items.fix: Use a jug blender and blend for at least 60 seconds. Pass the blended soup through a sieve for an extremely smooth result.

items.symptom: Mint flavour is too strong or medicinal
items.cause: Too much mint added to the blender.
items.fix: Start with 15 g rather than 20 g. You can always add a little more to the jug and blend again; you can't take it out.

content.text: Serve cold in summer as a chilled soup starter: make it, chill it for at least two hours, then thin with a little extra stock and serve with a spoonful of crème fraîche. Watercress can replace half the peas for a more peppery version.

Pea soup is one of the oldest in the British canon. Elizabethan recipes made it with fresh garden peas. The frozen version is a modern convenience. Frozen peas are picked and frozen within hours, which makes them sweeter and fresher than peas left in the pod for days.
